region_of_origin: China (Lanzhou / Northwest Chinese tradition, adapted for Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for sautéed spicy beef fillet specifically; FOODON_03302051 (beef dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for stir-fried spicy beef fillet exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Beef dish) is the closest available parent concept; no specific term for sautéed spicy beef fillet exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sautéed spicy beef fillet is approximately 200 g, representing a single main-course portion as served at a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables and sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; adapted with reference to USDA NDB No. 17158 (Beef, tenderloin, cooked, broiled) and USDA NDB No. 21065 (Beef, stir-fried with sauce)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for 'sautéed spicy beef fillet' exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef tenderloin (FDC ID 174036) and stir-fried beef with sauce (FDC ID 168164), weighted to reflect a spicy Chinese stir-fry preparation with soy sauce, chilli, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked beef to account for soy sauce and oyster sauce. Fat is moderately elevated to account for stir-fry cooking oil. Carbohydrate reflects cornstarch and sauce contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; stir-fried beef with mixed sauce analogous estimate; beef and mixed vegetable stir-fry GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sautéed spicy beef fillet. GI estimated at 35 based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for beef stir-fry with sauce (GI ~35, low category). The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content of the dish with minimal carbohydrate from cornstarch and sauce. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 3.6 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1040 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~1040 mg per serving), which represents approximately 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-the-side preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load (~3.6 per serving) due to high protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load (~3.6 per serving), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the sodium content from sauces warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.1 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~520 mg per 100 g) from stir-fry preparation with soy-based sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its moderate saturated fat content and high sodium load from soy and oyster sauces; choosing lean beef cuts and requesting reduced sauce can help lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sautéed Spicy Beef Fillet (香辣炒牛里脊) is a Chinese stir-fried dish featuring tender slices of beef fillet — typically tenderloin or sirloin — cooked rapidly over high heat in a wok with chilli peppers, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is characteristic of Chinese restaurant cooking in Singapore and is associated with Lanzhou and Northwest Chinese culinary traditions. The beef is typically velveted with cornstarch before cooking to achieve a silky, tender texture. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, and spicy, with umami depth from the soy and oyster sauces balanced by the heat of fresh or dried chillies. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 19.5 g protein, 9.8 g fat (3.1 g saturated), and 5.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high (~520 mg per 100 g) due to soy-based sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~35), and the glycemic load per serving is low (~3.6), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. The dish is rich in zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and niacin from the beef, and provides moderate amounts of potassium and phosphorus. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy or gluten allergies. A typical restaurant serving is approximately 200 g.

confidence_reason: No exact match for sautéed spicy beef fillet was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef tenderloin and stir-fried beef with sauce, adjusted for the spicy Chinese preparation style. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry precisely matches this dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this adapted record. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible analogous data existed. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked beef to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce contributions typical of Chinese stir-fry preparations. The halal status is marked false because the dish contains beef and the restaurant source (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) does not carry a confirmed halal certification in available public records; this should be verified with the restaurant directly. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for beef stir-fry with sauce; glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (35 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 3.6.
